A group that cannot leverage technology will lose share.
Eliane, I read the profile you sent. The company that worries you most is the incumbent, right?
It was, Paul. But the incumbent, which holds the biggest market share, has high overhead and thin margins, so it moves slowly.
Exactly. So the company that should interest you is the start-up whose valuation jumped last year.
Yes. Their competitive edge is technology that lowers the cost per cycle, and they opened a revenue stream, data and software, that no one else offers.
Good. And any group that cannot leverage that kind of technology will lose ground. So where does your clinic fit?
In a niche the big group ignores. The clinic that I am building does not need the most patients, it needs a margin it can sustain.
Smart. Before you sign with anyone, do the due diligence that protects you: compare their numbers against a real benchmark.
I will. And I will study the portfolio of services that made the leaders grow, so I copy what works and skip what does not.
That is a founder who reads her market, not just her medicine. The clinic that wins is the one that knows exactly where it competes.
The incumbent, which holds the biggest market share, moves slowly.
Their competitive edge is technology that lowers the cost per cycle.
I do the due diligence that every serious founder does.
The clinic that I am building has a niche it can defend.
We compare our numbers against a benchmark the industry trusts.

Hi Eliane, it is Paul. I have looked at the market profile you sent, and here is my quick read. The incumbent, which still holds the biggest market share, is not your real problem. Its overhead is high and its margins are thin, so it moves slowly. The company that should interest you is the start-up whose valuation jumped last year. Their competitive edge is a technology that lowers the cost of each cycle, and they have opened a revenue stream, data and software, that no one else offers. My advice is simple: do not try to beat the incumbent on size. Find the niche that you can defend, and do the due diligence that protects your margin. The clinic that wins is the one that knows exactly where it competes.

Hi team, it is Eliane. I want to recap what I learned reading our market this week. First, the leader, the incumbent that everyone watches, has the biggest market share, but its high overhead makes it slow. Second, the start-up whose funding we discussed has one clear competitive edge: technology that cuts the cost per cycle. Analysts who follow the sector say its new revenue stream is the future. Here is our takeaway: we will not compete on size. We will do the due diligence that serious founders do, compare ourselves against a real benchmark, and build a portfolio of services that gives us a margin we can sustain and a niche we can defend. That is the strategy which will carry us.
